Q1: Honestly, my study routine felt chaotic even when I tried to organize it. I'd set aside time to read through materials and take notes, but I always felt this underlying anxiety that I wasn't doing enough. I preferred studying late at night when my apartment was quiet, mostly reading and writing out summaries. With 17 courses to juggle and limited resources, I had to be strategic about what got my attention each week.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have stretches where I studied at consistent times, but then deadlines would pile up and everything went out the window. The anxiety of falling behind would kick in, and I'd end up cramming at odd hours. It wasn't sustainable, but with my stress levels, I couldn't seem to find a better rhythm.
Q3: Usually it was fear, honestly. Fear that I'd missed an announcement or a deadline. Sometimes I'd get a notification and my heart would race before I even logged in. Other times I'd force myself to check just to quiet that scared voice in my head telling me I was forgetting something important.
Q4: Not really, no. Logging in never felt natural or comfortable to me. It was more like a chore I dreaded but knew I had to do. Even when I tried to make it routine, the anxiety around it made me avoid it until absolutely necessary. The LMS felt more like a source of stress than a helpful tool.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since that matches how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and articles and work through them with my notes. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in ways that clicked for me, but I found the written course content most valuable.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos or watched them minimally. With limited internet and low resources, streaming wasn't always practical. Plus, I retain information better through reading and writing anyway. I'd skim video descriptions and find text alternatives when possible. The peer interaction features also went largely unused because reaching out felt overwhelming.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. I couldn't afford to waste energy on things that felt like busywork. If I could see how something would help my grade, I'd push through the anxiety and do it. Otherwise, my limited motivation made it hard to justify the effort.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps to me. Like, I knew they might help, but the thought of adding more to my plate when I was already scared and stressed made me freeze. Required activities had clear consequences, so I did them. Optional ones just added to my guilt when I couldn't bring myself to complete them.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could and work from printed notes or saved files. I have notebooks full of summaries and practice problems that the LMS would never know about. Given my limited resources, I couldn't always be online, so offline study was essential.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd sit with my printed materials for hours, re-reading and rewriting notes until concepts stuck. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also talk through problems out loud to myself or quiz myself from handwritten flashcards. My best learning happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading once, download it immediately, and then spend days working through it offline. My LMS activity would show one brief login, but I might have spent ten hours with that material over the following week. It was just more practical and less anxiety-inducing than staying logged in.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt impossible. When my anxiety spiked, even opening my laptop felt terrifying. Financial stress and competing responsibilities would pile up, and I'd feel paralyzed. During those times, I'd still be studying from my notes, but engaging with the actual course platform felt like too much.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was constant with my stress levels, and confusion about course expectations made everything harder. When I had work or other responsibilities competing for my time, the LMS was the first thing I'd cut because I could study offline instead. The platform became something I used only when absolutely necessary.
Q14: Many times. I'd scroll through discussion boards or click through modules without really absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with anxious thoughts, and I'd realize I'd spent twenty minutes staring at a page without reading it. Being logged in didn't mean I was actually present or learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly even though I knew participation mattered. The thought of putting my thoughts out there for others to see terrified me. I'd also avoid checking feedback on assignments because I was scared of criticism. The fear of negative responses made avoidance feel safer in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were the main thing that got me to actually log in. Without them, I probably would have done everything offline. As deadlines approached, my anxiety would spike, and I'd finally force myself onto the platform to submit work. It wasn't healthy, but that fear-driven urgency was often the only thing that overcame my avoidance.
Q17: Feedback affected me deeply, sometimes too deeply. Positive feedback would give me a brief moment of relief, but negative feedback could send me spiraling for days. I started avoiding checking my grades because the anticipation was unbearable. When I did get good feedback, it motivated me to keep going despite everything.
Q18: The structure helped in some ways because clear deadlines gave me external motivation I couldn't generate myself. But having 17 courses meant the structure was overwhelming. I appreciated when modules were organized logically, but the sheer volume made it hard to feel like I was ever on top of things.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement possible for me given my situation. But features that required constant online presence or real-time interaction were harder. The discussion requirements stressed me out because of my social anxiety. Clearer expectations upfront would have reduced some of my fear about whether I was doing enough.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually engaging. Or they could be searching for something specific and not finding it. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes clicking around is just a way of feeling like you're doing something when you're too overwhelmed to actually work.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS engagement was low, but I performed well because my real studying happened offline. I'd download materials and work through them thoroughly on my own. The system only saw my brief logins and submissions, not the hours of reading and writing I did away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone submitted work and maybe how often they logged in. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the offline learning. They can't measure understanding, anxiety, or effort. My logs would show low engagement, but my grades show I was learning—just not in ways the system could track.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just clicking through requirements. For me, engagement happened when I was alone with my notes, really thinking through concepts. It's about mental investment, not platform activity. You can be deeply engaged without ever touching an LMS.
Q24: The LMS would never show how scared I was throughout this course, or how much that fear both motivated and paralyzed me. It wouldn't show the mindfulness exercises I did just to calm down enough to study, or the hours of offline work that led to my grades. It can't capture the emotional cost of performing well despite everything working against me.
